content.text: A GenAI developer platform is infrastructure, not a feature. Teams building production systems depend on it for API keys, model access, evaluations, and prompt management. When the platform is unreliable or support is slow, teams build workarounds that fracture the ecosystem. A platform SLA and support model sets explicit expectations about availability, response times, and escalation paths — and holds the platform team accountable to them the same way it expects product teams to be accountable to their SLOs.

Not every platform component has the same availability requirement. The eval runner can tolerate a 30-minute outage if it only affects CI checks; the LLM gateway cannot tolerate 30 minutes if it is in the critical path of production services. Availability targets should reflect actual business impact of downtime.
Direct production impact; all model calls fail
New teams blocked; existing keys continue to work
Applications cannot fetch prompt versions; fall back to pinned cache
Prompt authoring blocked; no production impact if cache is warm
CI gates delayed or bypassed; no direct production impact
Developer portal unavailable; direct API access continues to work
New installs blocked; deployed applications unaffected

SLOs are measured per calendar month. A component's SLO is met when its uptime — defined as successful health-check responses divided by total health-check requests — is at or above the target. Degraded responses that complete successfully count as available; timeouts count as unavailable.
The support model has four tiers. Developers are expected to exhaust lower tiers before escalating. Each tier has a defined channel, a response SLA, and an hours commitment.

All common questions, error codes, and operational procedures are documented in TechDocs and linked from the Backstage catalog entry for each platform component. Tier 0 has no SLA because it is always available. The platform team's obligation is to keep documentation current — stale documentation that sends developers to Tier 1 for answered questions is a maintenance failure, not a support event.
Error code reference: every SDK error type with cause, diagnostic steps, and resolution.
Runbook per component: how to verify health, check quota usage, rotate an API key, re-run a failed eval.
FAQ: the 20 questions most commonly asked in #genai-platform-help, updated monthly.
Changelog: every breaking change, deprecation notice, and upgrade guide.

The #genai-platform-help Slack channel is the primary Tier 1 channel. A rotating TC answers questions during business hours. The TC on-call is a weekly rotation; the schedule is published in the channel topic and the Backstage component.

First response within 1 business day. Resolution is best-effort — not all questions have a platform team answer. If no resolution within 2 business days, the question is converted to a JIRA ticket and escalated to Tier 2.

Tier 1 also includes the internal Stack Overflow instance (tag: genai-platform) and GitHub Discussions on the platform repository. Both are indexed and searchable, so frequently-answered questions build up a permanent knowledge base that reduces repeat questions.
Issues that cannot be resolved in Tier 1 are escalated to a JIRA ticket with the label
. The TC on-call triages new tickets during business hours and assigns them a priority based on impact.
Complete outage of a production-critical component; data loss risk
Significant degradation affecting multiple teams; no effective workaround
One team blocked; workaround exists but is painful

The TC on-call is responsible for: acknowledging new tickets within the SLA, providing an initial diagnosis, and setting a resolution timeline. For P1 and higher, the TC posts a status message in #genai-platform-status every 30 minutes until resolution.

A P0 or SEV1 incident triggers the war room protocol. An incident commander is paged via PagerDuty. The incident commander's responsibilities differ from the fix engineer's: they coordinate communication, set a timeline, and decide when to escalate to vendor support — they do not necessarily write the fix.

# Incident Response Checklist — P0/SEV1

## First 15 minutes
- [ ] Incident commander acknowledges PagerDuty alert
- [ ] Post in #genai-platform-incidents: "Investigating [component] issue since [time]. IC: @handle."
- [ ] Open war-room channel: #incident-YYYYMMDD-NN
- [ ] Assess blast radius: which components, which teams, what user impact

## First 30 minutes
- [ ] Post customer impact statement in #genai-platform-status (visible to all engineers)
- [ ] Confirm all TCs aware; assign fix engineer(s)
- [ ] Determine if vendor (AWS Bedrock / Azure OpenAI / Anthropic) involvement needed
- [ ] Post first 15-min status update

## During incident
- [ ] Status update every 15 minutes in #incident channel
- [ ] Update status page (internal) with current state
- [ ] Document all actions taken with timestamps in incident doc

## Resolution
- [ ] Confirm fix verified in production (health checks passing)
- [ ] Post all-clear in #genai-platform-incidents and #genai-platform-status
- [ ] Schedule post-mortem within 48 hours
- [ ] Create friction item in backlog for root cause fix
The TC on-call rotation covers two roles with different expectations:
24/7 for P0/P1; business hours for P2–P4
First responder to all alerts; triages new support tickets; owns status updates
Backup if primary is unreachable; joins war room for P0
Weekly rotation, offset by one week from primary
Monitors #genai-platform-help; answers Tier 1 questions; reviews new GitHub Discussion posts

The on-call schedule is published 4 weeks in advance. Swaps require 48 hours notice and both parties' acknowledgement in the schedule tool. Persistent on-call overload (one TC covering more than 1-in-3 weeks) is a staffing signal that triggers a TC expansion conversation.
endpoint that the monitoring stack polls every 30 seconds. Availability is computed from these health checks. An SLO breach alert fires when the 30-day rolling availability drops below the target.

# monitoring/slo_tracker.py
# Compute rolling monthly availability from Prometheus health check data.

import httpx
from datetime import timedelta

PROMETHEUS_URL = "http://prometheus.internal:9090"

COMPONENTS = {
    "llm-gateway":          {"slo": 0.999, "query": 'avg_over_time(up{job="llm-gateway"}[30d])'},
    "api-key-provisioning": {"slo": 0.995, "query": 'avg_over_time(up{job="api-key-service"}[30d])'},
    "prompt-registry":      {"slo": 0.995, "query": 'avg_over_time(up{job="prompt-registry"}[30d])'},
    "eval-runner":          {"slo": 0.990, "query": 'avg_over_time(up{job="eval-runner"}[30d])'},
}

def check_slos() -> list[dict]:
    results = []
    for component, cfg in COMPONENTS.items():
        resp = httpx.get(
            f"{PROMETHEUS_URL}/api/v1/query",
            params={"query": cfg["query"]},
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()["data"]["result"]
        if not data:
            results.append({"component": component, "availability": None, "status": "no-data"})
            continue
        availability = float(data[0]["value"][1])
        error_budget_minutes = (1 - cfg["slo"]) * 30 * 24 * 60
        consumed_minutes = (cfg["slo"] - availability) * 30 * 24 * 60
        results.append({
            "component": component,
            "availability": round(availability, 5),
            "slo": cfg["slo"],
            "status": "ok" if availability >= cfg["slo"] else "breach",
            "error_budget_minutes": round(error_budget_minutes, 1),
            "consumed_minutes": round(max(consumed_minutes, 0), 1),
        })
    return results

if __name__ == "__main__":
    for r in check_slos():
        status_icon = "✅" if r["status"] == "ok" else "🚨"
        print(f"{status_icon} {r['component']}: {r['availability']:.3%} "
              f"(SLO: {r['slo']:.3%}, budget: {r['error_budget_minutes']}min, "
              f"consumed: {r['consumed_minutes']}min)")

When a component breaches its SLO for the calendar month, two things happen automatically: a P1 friction item is created in the backlog, and the next quarterly planning cycle must include a roadmap slot for remediation. This rule prevents the team from treating SLO breaches as acceptable background noise.
The post-mortem for the breach is written within 5 business days and published to the TechDocs page for the component.
The root cause is documented in the friction item with a severity of P1.
The remediation epic enters the next quarter's planning cycle as a reliability budget item — it cannot be displaced by new-capability work.
If the same component breaches its SLO in two consecutive months, the TC Council reviews whether the SLO target is realistic or whether the component needs a dedicated reliability quarter.
The support model is bilateral. Consumer teams have obligations that make the support relationship work:
Use the pinned SDK version in CHANGELOG
Unpinned or downgraded SDK versions may reproduce known bugs; the team cannot support versions beyond the current and one prior major
Include reproducible steps when filing a support ticket
"It's broken" tickets cannot be diagnosed; a minimal reproduction (config, input, full error message) reduces resolution time by 60–80%
Support tickets for questions answered in the FAQ or error code reference are closed with a documentation link
Test in staging before production rollout
Production incidents caused by untested configuration changes are deprioritised behind genuine platform failures
Breaking changes, deprecations, and planned maintenance are announced with ≥ 2 weeks notice; teams that miss the announcement cannot claim urgent support

Reciprocity is the mechanism. The platform team commits to SLAs and transparent communication. Consumer teams commit to using the platform as documented and providing the information needed to resolve issues quickly. Without both sides, the support relationship degrades into a one-sided escalation culture where every issue becomes a P1 regardless of actual impact.
Every P0 incident and every SLO breach triggers a blameless post-mortem. The post-mortem template is stored in the platform repository and linked from each incident ticket.

# Post-Mortem: [Component] [Brief Description] — [Date]

**Severity:** P0 / SEV1
**Duration:** HH:MM (from first alert to resolution)
**Incident Commander:** @handle
**Fix Engineer(s):** @handle, @handle
**Status:** Resolved / Monitoring

## Impact Summary
- Components affected: [list]
- Teams affected: [list, or "all teams" if broad]
- User-visible impact: [one paragraph: what developers couldn't do]
- Business impact: [model calls failed, prod deploys blocked, etc.]

## Timeline (UTC)
| Time  | Event |
|-------|-------|
| HH:MM | Alert fired / first report |
| HH:MM | IC acknowledged |
| HH:MM | Root cause identified |
| HH:MM | Mitigation applied |
| HH:MM | Service restored |
| HH:MM | All-clear posted |

## Root Cause
[Technical explanation of what failed and why. Be specific: not "config error"
but "The gateway's health check endpoint returned 200 even when the upstream
Bedrock endpoint was returning 503, because the health check did not exercise
the upstream connection."]

## Contributing Factors
[What conditions made this possible or worse?]

## What Went Well
[Be genuine — this is not a formality.]

## Action Items
| Action | Owner | Due date | Friction item |
|--------|-------|----------|---------------|
| Fix health check to test upstream | @alice | +1 week | friction-20260701-007 |
| Add synthetic canary probing upstream endpoint | @bob | +2 weeks | friction-20260701-008 |

## Lessons Learned
[What would have prevented this? What would have reduced impact?]
Manual ticket triage at scale is expensive. A small amount of automation in the Tier 1 and Tier 2 channels reduces TC on-call load significantly and improves response time for common issues.

A bot monitors #genai-platform-help and auto-responds to messages that match known error patterns. The bot response includes the relevant documentation link and asks if the solution resolved the issue. If not, it opens a JIRA ticket automatically.

# support_bot/slack_handler.py
import re
from slack_sdk import WebClient

client = WebClient(token="<vault:genai-platform/slack-bot-token>")

# Pattern → doc link mapping (extend as new errors are documented)
ERROR_PATTERNS = [
    (r"RetryExhaustedError",       "https://docs.internal/genai/sdk/errors#retry-exhausted"),
    (r"AuthenticationError",       "https://docs.internal/genai/sdk/errors#auth"),
    (r"QuotaExceededError",        "https://docs.internal/genai/gateway/quotas"),
    (r"PromptRef.*not found",      "https://docs.internal/genai/prompt-registry/versioning"),
    (r"eval.*no test cases",       "https://docs.internal/genai/eval-runner/test-case-format"),
    (r"Invalid API key format",    "https://docs.internal/genai/api-keys/format"),
]

def on_message(event: dict) -> None:
    text = event.get("text", "")
    channel = event["channel"]
    thread_ts = event.get("ts")

    for pattern, doc_url in ERROR_PATTERNS:
        if re.search(pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE):
            client.chat_postMessage(
                channel=channel,
                thread_ts=thread_ts,
                text=(
                    f":robot_face: I spotted `{pattern}` in your message. "
                    f"This error is documented here: {doc_url}\n\n"
                    "Does the runbook resolve your issue? "
                    "React :white_check_mark: if yes, :x: if no (I'll open a ticket)."
                ),
            )
            return   # only respond to the first matching pattern

def on_reaction_added(event: dict) -> None:
    if event["reaction"] == "x":
        thread_ts = event["item"]["ts"]
        # Retrieve the original message text from thread
        result = client.conversations_history(
            channel=event["item"]["channel"], latest=thread_ts, limit=1, inclusive=True
        )
        original_text = result["messages"][0]["text"] if result["messages"] else ""
        create_jira_ticket(
            summary=original_text[:200],
            reporter=event["user"],
            source=f"slack-{thread_ts}",
        )

When a support ticket is filed from the Slack bot, the ticket's initial priority is set automatically based on error rate data from Prometheus. A spike in the relevant error counter in the last 24 hours escalates the default priority.

# support_bot/jira_integration.py
import httpx

PROMETHEUS_URL = "http://prometheus.internal:9090"

def error_rate_24h(error_type: str) -> float:
    q = f'increase(genai_sdk_errors_total{{error_type="{error_type}"}}[24h])'
    resp = httpx.get(f"{PROMETHEUS_URL}/api/v1/query", params={"query": q})
    resp.raise_for_status()
    results = resp.json()["data"]["result"]
    return float(results[0]["value"][1]) if results else 0.0

def auto_priority(error_type: str) -> str:
    rate = error_rate_24h(error_type)
    if rate > 500:  return "P1"
    if rate > 100:  return "P2"
    if rate > 20:   return "P3"
    return "P4"

def create_jira_ticket(summary: str, reporter: str, source: str) -> str:
    error_match = re.search(r"(\w+Error)", summary)
    error_type  = error_match.group(1) if error_match else "Unknown"
    priority    = auto_priority(error_type)

    payload = {
        "fields": {
            "project":     {"key": "GENAI"},
            "summary":     summary[:200],
            "issuetype":   {"name": "Bug"},
            "labels":      ["platform-support"],
            "priority":    {"name": {"P1":"Highest","P2":"High","P3":"Medium","P4":"Low"}[priority]},
            "description": f"Source: {source}\nAuto-priority: {priority}\nError rate 24h: {error_rate_24h(error_type):.0f}",
        }
    }
    resp = httpx.post(
        "https://jira.internal/rest/api/3/issue",
        json=payload,
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer <vault:genai-platform/jira-token>"},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["key"]
The platform SLA covers the platform components. It does not cover every scenario involving those components. Clear exclusions prevent support disputes.
Upstream model provider outages (Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, Anthropic)
The gateway routes to upstream providers; provider availability is outside platform control. The gateway will surface errors with provider-specific codes.
File with the upstream vendor; monitor provider status pages
Whether a prompt produces the desired output is an application concern, not a platform concern. The prompt registry stores and versions prompts; it does not validate their quality.
Eval runner for systematic quality measurement; advisory group for prompt engineering office hours
Unsupported SDK versions (> 1 major version behind)
Supporting old versions indefinitely blocks the team from fixing known bugs. Supported versions are current + one prior major.
Upgrade to the current major version; see CHANGELOG for migration guide
Custom infrastructure deployed outside the platform template
Teams that fork the scaffold template and diverge significantly are responsible for their own infrastructure. The platform team can provide guidance but cannot debug arbitrary Terraform.
Platform office hours; inner-source contribution if the customisation is broadly useful
The platform team provides security controls at the platform boundary (auth, quota, secret management). Application-level vulnerabilities are the application team's responsibility.
The support model is a product. It has metrics, and those metrics are reviewed quarterly alongside the DevEx survey to determine whether the support system is working or creating bottlenecks.
% of tickets receiving first response within SLA per priority
Investigate on-call load; consider adding a second community duty TC
% of #genai-platform-help questions answered with a documentation link (no TC intervention)
Identify and document top unresolved question categories
Median time from ticket creation to status=resolved for P2 tickets
Review stuck tickets; if systemic, add to quarterly planning as reliability budget
% of P0/SLO-breach events with a completed post-mortem within 5 days
Blocked post-mortems are escalated to platform lead; missed deadline is a process failure
Review low-rated tickets for resolution quality and communication

A platform SLA and support model defines the contract between the platform team and the engineering organisation. Availability SLOs set explicit reliability targets per component, matched to the actual business impact of downtime. The four-tier support model — self-serve documentation, community Slack, platform team tickets, and war-room incidents — routes developer requests to the right level without overwhelming the platform team. Blameless post-mortems for every SLO breach close the reliability loop: root causes become friction items, friction items become roadmap commits, and the platform improves predictably over time. For consumer teams, the model is bilateral: the platform team commits to SLAs, and consumer teams commit to using the platform as documented and providing reproducible issue reports.
